1.synthesis:
Deuteronomy 10:1–11 narrates YHWH's restoration of the broken covenant through the replacement tablets — the Torah's account of divine recommitment after catastrophic covenant failure. YHWH's command to Moses is remarkable in its simplicity: 'Carve two tablets of stone like the first, and come up to me on the mountain and make an ark of wood. And I will write on the tablets the words that were on the first tablets, which you broke, and you shall put them in the ark' (vv. 1–2). The identical content of the replacement tablets — the same words as those broken after the golden calf — signals covenant continuity despite rupture: YHWH's word stands, and the covenant is re-enacted on YHWH's initiative, not Israel's.

Moses executed the command: he carved the tablets, made the acacia wood ark, ascended the mountain for the second forty-day fast, and YHWH wrote on the tablets exactly as on the first (vv. 3–5). The tablets were then placed in the ark — the wooden chest becoming the permanent housing of the covenant's written terms. The parenthetical notices that follow — Aaron's death at Moserah, Eleazar's succession to the priesthood, the Levites' appointment to carry the ark and stand before YHWH to bless in his name (vv. 6–9) — locate the covenant's institutional mediation in both the priestly line (Aaron/Eleazar) and the tribe of Levi. The section closes with the outcome of Moses's second forty days: 'YHWH listened to me that time also, and YHWH was unwilling to destroy you. And YHWH said to me, "Arise, go on your journey before the people, so that they may go in and possess the land"' (vv. 10–11). The intercession succeeded; the journey resumed.
1.historical_context: The 'ark of acacia wood' (aron atsei shittim) is the modest portable chest constructed for the tablets before the more elaborate ark described in Exodus 25 was built. Deuteronomy's account emphasizes the simplicity of the container — Moses made it himself from a single wood species — to focus attention on the tablets it housed rather than the ark's craftsmanship. The death of Aaron at Moserah (v. 6) corresponds to the account in Numbers 33:37–38, which places Aaron's death at Mount Hor; Moserah was apparently a nearby location or region name. Eleazar's succession maintained the Aaronic priesthood as a dynastic institution. The Levites' appointment to carry the ark and bless in YHWH's name is the permanent institutional arrangement established at the covenant's restoration.
1.literary_structure: The unit moves through three stages: the restoration command (vv. 1–2), the execution of that command (vv. 3–5), and the institutional arrangements that accompany the restored covenant (vv. 6–11). The parenthetical priestly and Levitical notices between the tablets narrative and the outcome of Moses's intercession show Deuteronomy weaving covenant content (the tablets) and covenant institution (the priesthood and the Levites) into a single account of restoration. YHWH's word, the priestly line, and the Levitical ministry are all restored together.
1.theological_application: The replacement tablets enact the theology of covenant restoration: the covenant is not cancelled by Israel's failure but renewed on divine initiative. YHWH does not demand a new covenant after the golden calf — he demands the same words on new stone, placed in an ark Israel makes and carries. The permanent principle is that covenant renewal after failure always bears the marks of both judgment (the first tablets were broken; Moses burned, ground, and scattered the calf) and grace (YHWH writes again; the journey resumes). The institutional provisions — the Levites appointed to carry the covenant's housing — show that sustained covenant relationship requires mediatorial structure. YHWH provides both the content (the written word) and the means of its carriage (the tribe set apart to bear it).
1.christology: The replacement tablets — written by YHWH, housed in a wooden ark, carried by a consecrated tribe — anticipate the NT's pattern of incarnate word mediated through a covenant community. Colossians 2:13–14 describes what happens to the written code at the cross: 'the record of debt that stood against us with its legal demands... he set aside, nailing it to the cross.' The stone tablets that accused Israel's failure are superseded not by replacement tablets but by the cross — the legal demands fulfilled, not renewed. 2 Corinthians 3:3 extends the ark-typology in a different direction: 'you are a letter from Christ delivered by us, written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts.' The new covenant's tablets are human hearts; the writer is the Spirit; the carrier is not the Levites but the apostolic community.

12.synthesis:
The great summary question of vv. 12–22 — mah YHWH Eloheicha sho'el me'immach ('And now, Israel, what does YHWH your God ask of you?') (v. 12) — is the Deuteronomic reduction of covenant obligation to its essential form. The answer comes as five infinitives: 'to fear YHWH your God, to walk in all his ways, to love him, to serve YHWH your God with all your heart and with all your soul, and to keep the commandments and statutes of YHWH, which I am commanding you today for your good' (vv. 12–13). The quintet — fear, walk, love, serve, keep — covers the full spectrum of covenant relationship: the interior disposition (fear and love), the habitual orientation (walk in his ways), the devotional act (serve), and the behavioral expression (keep the commandments). Each informs and requires the others; none can be isolated.

The cosmic scope of YHWH's ownership provides the ground for exclusive covenant loyalty: 'Behold, to YHWH your God belong heaven and the heaven of heavens, the earth with all that is in it. Yet YHWH set his heart in love on your fathers and chose their offspring after them, you above all peoples, as you are this day' (vv. 14–15). The one who owns all chose few — the election of Israel is not YHWH's limitation but the gracious specificity of the one who possesses everything. The covenant implication follows: 'Circumcise therefore the foreskin of your heart, and be no longer stubborn' (v. 16). The qesheh oref (stiffness) diagnosed in chapter 9 has its surgical remedy named here — heart-circumcision, the removal of the resistance that makes covenant obedience so labored.

YHWH's character is articulated as the ground of social ethics: 'For YHWH your God is God of gods and Lord of lords, the great, the mighty, and the awesome God, who is not partial and takes no bribe. He executes justice for the fatherless and the widow, and loves the sojourner, giving him food and clothing. Love the sojourner, therefore, for you were sojourners in the land of Egypt' (vv. 17–19). The care for the marginalized — fatherless, widow, stranger — flows directly from YHWH's own character and is commanded because Israel has experienced being the alien. The section closes with the Exodus-to-present trajectory: from 70 souls into Egypt (v. 22) to a nation as numerous as the stars of heaven — the patriarchal promise fulfilled before their eyes.
12.historical_context: The five-fold covenant summary of vv. 12–13 is formally similar to the prophetic covenant summaries that Deuteronomy influenced — particularly Micah 6:8 ('do justice, love kindness, and walk humbly with your God') and Amos 5:14 ('seek good, not evil'). The 'God of gods and Lord of lords' formula (v. 17) is the Deuteronomic expression of YHWH's absolute sovereignty over all divine or semi-divine beings — it appears again in Psalm 136:2–3 and Revelation 19:16. The 70 souls who went down to Egypt (v. 22) echoes Genesis 46:27; the contrast with the 'stars of heaven' population fulfills the Abrahamic promise of Genesis 15:5 — the very promise sworn to Abraham whose oath grounds the conquest.
12.literary_structure: The unit moves from covenant summary (vv. 12–13) to theological grounding (vv. 14–15) to the heart-circumcision command (v. 16) to YHWH's character as model for social ethics (vv. 17–19) to covenant summary and Exodus-to-present closure (vv. 20–22). The five-fold covenant obligation opens the unit; the nation-as-stars-of-heaven fulfillment closes it — the answer to 'what does YHWH ask?' is bounded by the evidence of what YHWH has already done.
12.theological_application: The five-fold covenant summary — fear, walk, love, serve, keep — is Deuteronomy's answer to the perennial question of what faithfulness requires. The ordering is significant: fear and love (interior dispositions) precede walking and keeping (behavioral expressions), and service integrates both. The heart-circumcision command makes explicit that the problem is not primarily behavioral but dispositional: Israel's qesheh oref (stiffness) is the internal resistance that produces external disobedience. The sojourner-care command grounds social ethics not in abstract principle but in remembered experience and in YHWH's own character — Israel must love the alien because YHWH loves the alien, and because Israel was the alien. Every ethical obligation in this unit traces back to YHWH's character and Israel's story.
12.christology: The heart-circumcision of v. 16 is the Mosaic anticipation of the new covenant promise in Deuteronomy 30:6 ('YHWH your God will circumcise your heart') and its NT fulfillment in Romans 2:29: 'But a Jew is one inwardly, and circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the letter.' The stiffness Moses diagnosed as Israel's constitutional problem is the problem the Spirit addresses in new covenant believers — not commanding a circumcised heart but effecting it. The 'God of gods and Lord of lords' title of v. 17 is transferred to Christ in Revelation 17:14 ('Lord of lords and King of kings') and Revelation 19:16 — the one who executes justice for the fatherless and widow is the one who returns as sovereign judge to make all things right.
